title: Uganda Asset Protection & Liquidation (DEMO - Historical)
description: Asset management during forced expulsion. The 1972 'Economic War' resulted in mass confiscation of Asian-owned businesses and properties.
items: Liquidate property discreetly - official channels were compromised. Buyers knew sellers were desperate, prices were exploitative.
Transfer money through unofficial channels - banks froze accounts or limited withdrawals to token amounts. Hawala networks were critical.
Convert local currency to stable foreign currency or gold - Ugandan Shilling was unstable. British Pounds, US Dollars, or gold were preferred.
Limit of £50 cash allowed to be taken out per person - anything above was confiscated at checkpoints or airport
Jewelry and small valuables - easier to conceal and transport than cash. Sewn into clothing or hidden in luggage linings.
Document all confiscated property - for future restitution claims, though most were never honored
Business closure notices - required by law but often used as pretext for asset seizure
Settle debts where possible - creditors had no recourse after expulsion, but maintaining honor mattered to some
Do NOT advertise desperation - predatory buyers, informants, and criminals targeted those known to be leaving
Trust no one with full picture - compartmentalize information about finances
